QF.id: IV Strategies Using Interest Rate Derivatives_74

question.text: A proprietary trading desk wants to profit from an expected steepening of the yield curve (short rates falling relative to long rates). Which spread trade would they execute?

answer.text: Buy short-term bond futures (or T-Bill futures) and sell long-term bond futures
Sell short-term bond futures and buy long-term bond futures
Buy both short-term and long-term bond futures
Sell both short-term and long-term bond futures

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Yield curve steepening means short rates fall (short bond prices rise) and long rates rise relatively (long bond prices fall relatively). Buy short-term futures (profits from short rate decline/price rise) and sell long-term futures (profits from long rate rise/price fall). **The Trap:** The reverse trade profits from flattening. Buying both is a parallel bull bet. Selling both is a parallel bear bet. **Cross-Exam Context:** Yield curve trades using futures are a sophisticated application tested in Series IV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Curve steepener: buy front, sell back. Curve flattener: sell front, buy back. Always think in terms of which end benefits.
